transcript.text: Thank Good morning.
Thank you.
you.
So we're just So we're just about Fathom.
All right, let's get started.
Everyone can see my screen?
Perfect.
Thumbs up from Jen.
All right.
Do this.
So we have a couple of new hires today.
We'll do intros.
We'll look at new logos, lots of new logos since last time.
Quick pipeline update.
Oh, I forgot to add, we're going to do a Q2 review just of high level metrics.
And then Marcel is going to do a presentation on the end about one of the new docs he wrote.
So let's start with hires.
I don't know where your name went.
There we go.
Nico, do you want to kick off?
Bob, you can just unmute, tell us about where you live, your role, and maybe what you like to do outside of work.
Yeah.
Hello, everyone.
I'm Nico.
I live in Uruguay, Montevideo, which is right next to Argentina, here in South America.
I joined last week, and I'm on the client ops engineering team.
I've been learning a lot and been reading a lot of the stuff in Notion.
Super excited that you joined, and apart from work, I'm taking flying lessons.
I'm about to have my flying license for visual reference flights.
So, yeah, happy to be here.
Cool.
That's interesting.
And Katia, or Katia, I forget which one you prefer.
Yeah, it's Katia.
know that makes it easier for everyone to know.
My name is probably super confusing to pronounce, but I'm super excited to
Good be here.
I'm, I think, maybe the first marketing designer that we've got.
I'm just really excited to help dive in whatever I can in design.
I'm based in Springfield, Virginia, pretty close to Washington, D.C.
I've been in Virginia mostly all my life, but I did used to live in Bali, Indonesia.
That's where I'm from.
Outside of work, I think I've mentioned it before, but I'm a huge nerd.
I game a lot.
I try to stay up to date on all things tech.
Just because.
But yeah, that's pretty much it.
I have a dog named Mindy.
I don't know if we have, like, a pet's channel, but I'd love to share pictures of her one day.
I don't know if there is.
That's a good question.
Awesome.
Well, thank you both.
We're excited to have you on the team.
Let's jump into Q2 results.
Marcella, you want to do this or you want me to take it?
Okay, I'm going to do it.
So I'm going to start sharing these, probably monthly actually will be more helpful for everybody, just a snapshot of numbers and where we're at.
Sorry, I can take it, we reviewed it in the room, can you guys hear us okay?
Yeah, do you want to do it?
Yeah, I can take it, yeah.
Okay.
It's a bit hard to hear you, I don't know if it's just me though.
Can others hear you?
Is it because it's like...
Now it's better, stand right there.
Okay, okay, let me get you closer to this, is this better?
Yeah.
Okay, all right, perfect, thanks.
Cool, so quick summary of Q2, we ended at about 7.5 million in revenue run rate, so overall, really strong growth, but at the same time, I think there's kind of two sides to the story, right?
Like one side is, I think we're learning a lot, and we're...
We're building a lot, and we close out a lot of amazing clients.

On the other side, I think there's a lot of good logos we lost, some logos that we probably are non-regrettable that we lost as well, and I think we're addressing a lot of these things, but that's definitely, if you think about across our company, I think the only number that concerns me is really churn and retention overall, right?

And I think there's a couple of things we can talk through, and then I'll come back to kind of like the positives as well, but it's become very clear as we're going through these strategy spreads that we weren't putting as much effort as we could have in getting the right inputs from customers, right?

And so we just see when you do just a kickoff, an hour kickoff, there's no way you can know everything there is to know about somebody's business, about their customers, about their audiences, about what content, their strategy, and everything else, right?

And so as we're going through this in week one or two, and now we're doing like hours of deep dive, going into the product and understanding that at a deeper level, and then doing a much better job in creating these artifacts, it's nine a day, how much more insight.

And there's this kind of like reaction when you get to a meeting with a customer early on, and you say something that's an insight that maybe either they didn't have about their business, or that they're shocked how much you know about their business, and it immediately builds that level of trust, right?
And then from that point on, as we're thinking through how to help them, it just becomes a lot more useful.

The other part that, yeah, exactly, like Juergen said, like the best hire mentality, and so like, we're just seeing where there's a customer, for instance, like Biologica in the healthcare space, Aida's been leading the strategy sprint there, and it's just insane how...
The quality of the content we're generating, how much positive feedback we've gotten from those customers in a very, like, tricky space, right?
And so the other piece, too, like, we've gotten a lot of feedback.
I've been doing these listening tours with clients, and honestly, like, when we send customers a copy and paste of Surfer SEO or ChatDBT, that's when we break trust with them, right?
And I think a lot of clients have seen that firsthand where they know it's, like, they can tell, right?

And so now with our platform with Atlas and a lot of the work we're doing, we can make sure that we, every one of this company is actually using our workflows and using all that we build in order to improve the outputs for clients and that we can evaluate those outputs as well, right?
So those are, like, some of the kind of takeaways from there.
And then on the positive side, it's pretty insane the logos we closed just in the last few weeks.
We'll cover them, but there are.
Really amazing logos, and we have very, very, very happy customers as well, right?

And so we're starting to kind of see a trend where customers that are either Series A or Pre-Series A, no marketing team, you know, pivoting, those are the kinds of customers that when we lose, there's more we could have done.
But also, like, I think it, you know, they're still trying to find themselves, where when we go put the work, like we did with, let's say, like abnormal security, like they're very happy now, right?
And so we see customers like that, that love working with the team, and they're talking about upsells as well, right?
And most recently, too, a really exciting update, I think you all saw we closed Surge AI, that was a three-day deal at $60,000 a month, plus expenses.
And so really, really exciting, and I think there is also a bit of co-marketing, where we're closely associating our company and our brand.
With their brand and company as well, and it's like super beneficial to us.

They got to a billion in ARR last year with about 50 full-time employees and managing a marketplace of over 100,000 experts and labelers that are working on, you know, human preference data for a lot of these AI labs.

And so it's pretty insane, like, that it's just a lot of the interactions we've had with them just validated when you build the right way, how much leverage we can have in building from first principles, right?
How can you possibly have 50 people managing a billion in revenue and 100,000 people?
And so that's really, really exciting.
Overall, on cash balance, we ended the quarter with a little over 10 million in cash and burned a little under 2 million.
And so if you think about it, overall, like, burning 2 million in order to grow from, you know, call it 3 at the end of last year.

That gives us, like, a really, really strong burn multiple of less than 0.5, and so what that means is, like, it takes 50 cents or less to generate $1 of incremental run rate for us, and so that's very positive, that's very, very efficient, and our plan is to, as we continue to grow and continue to get more efficient, is for that burn not to increase either.

So, we're in an overall really good place, but a lot of work ahead of us to make sure we're winning the hearts and minds of our customers, and they are really, really excited to continue to work with us.
So, anything you want to add, Bridget?
Nope.
I think you covered it.
22 countries is also pretty freaking cool.
That is cool.
Does anybody have any questions initially on any of these numbers?
I didn't see anything in the chat, but feel free to drop questions as we go, if you'd like.
I just dropped one.
Sorry.
Do we have any document with the off-boarding of the churned clients to understand why they left?
We don't, but we're going to put one together in the next two weeks for our next board meeting, so happy to share that.
We have one for clients up until April, but I need to build it out through July, so we can definitely share that afterwards.
There's also a Slack channel called Churning and Learning or something, where you can follow along some reasons, but we'll be more consistent about that.
Cool, thanks.
All right, let's jump into, it took me a while to do this, there's so many logos, my ugly logo slide.
Marcel, you want to tell us about some of the new clients?
Yeah, I'll cover some, and Kyle can cover a few as well.
SentinelOne and Okta have been in the works for quite a while.
I think we have a nice little niche here between normal, Okta, SentinelOne, we should go get.
guys time.
time.
Ever secured a company on the planet.
so, really, really exciting to be working with a few more here.
And Sentinel One is a pretty large company.
took forever to go through procurement.
It was, you know, a fun process, I'm sure, for sales and team.
But we got it through.
Literally, they gave a verbal, like, I don't know, three months ago.
It's just like, we want to work with you.
Can we start next week?
And it's been like three months since, you know.
Search is the biggest data, AI research lab, providing data to most of the AI labs out there.
Most of their customers are, you know, Meta, Google, XAI, Anthropic.
If anyone uses Anthropic code and why Anthropic is, I think, probably the best model right now for coding, a lot of it is because of Search and the data that they've created or produced.
And you're like, really.
Complex data, and so it's been a really fun, fun experience.

We're producing this really cool documentary for them, essentially, and we just shared parts of it with them this week, and they're super excited, and then we're going to do a research blog for them as well, and this is really like us helping them tell their story, so we're really excited, and then do you want to cover outreach, you to grab?

Yeah, sure, so outreach, if you're not familiar, one of the biggest platforms out there for sales enablement, really recognizable brand in the SaaS space, you know, helping teams manage things like their outbound efforts, email, nurturing their prospects, et cetera, there's a variety of different use cases that SaaS companies will leverage them for.
Prior to GrowthX, I worked at a bunch of different SaaS companies, I think almost every one of them had outreach or some similar competitor.
Which is quite common, so this is an amazing logo.
And Monograph's super interesting, definitely a bit different than some of our other customers.
It is basically like a comprehensive tool for architects and engineers to manage projects, invoicing, budgeting, etc., just all that goes into running their business.
They mainly focus on SMB-sized architecture firms, and that's a bit of their sweet spot.
And so they didn't have a ton of content yet.
They haven't really, they've kind of had some, you know, a bit of like shifting and turnover and marketing, and this is a good opportunity for us to lay a good foundation for the organic growth.
So very excited about all of these.
And then on the next slide, a few other great ones in the late stage right now.
Udbe, this would be a fun one.
We're talking about spinning up a newsletter for them.
This would be an awesome opportunity just to show off just working with an amazing, well-known brand, but also doing something a bit different.
A few others in here.
Ambien should be closing today, tomorrow.
It's an interesting tool that combines AI with security, like helping people understand threats that may be happening on campuses or workplaces, etc.
I actually have talked a few of these, and Roar is somewhat similar.

They have a bit of a different play where there's like a device angle that they lean into, but there's this interesting blend now of physical security and keeping places safe and how you bring AI for detection alongside of that.
Just been seeing more and more of that.
And some other good security things in the mix as well.
But for time's sake, I'm going go through all of these.
Actually had a couple good combos yesterday, too, that will likely be on the slide in the not-too-distant future.
Cool.
Thank you.
All right, Marcel.
I think you wanted to talk about this new human AI framework.
Yeah, yeah.

We dropped a link in case folks haven't read that or seen the link that I shared, but I've been super inspired by just connecting with Surge and the team and just kind of seeing how they built the company and just being able to scale as much as they have and how much they essentially focus on quality.

And so that kind of inspired me a little bit to try to write something that helps us think through the different layers and the different roles that we all can play, and especially on the delivery side, right?
And so the way to kind of think about it is, like, we have three different layers here that...
Yeah.
Yeah.
Like in a company, right?
There's the service layer, which is what our customers care about.
Most customers, if we talk to them, for the most part, they'll say, hey, I actually don't care how you get this done.
As long as you get it done, it's efficient and it's really high quality, right?
And so that is the service layer.
That's what we promised, that outcomes we're accountable for.
And hopefully that will drive the right business results for them and the right business impact, right?
Then if you think about how the AI workflows is, where we're hopefully having AI and this combination of AI and different APIs and different processes, like, hopefully handle 80 to 90% of the work.
And it's not because we want to only automate and streamline and be more efficient, but also to make sure, like, we are going deeper, right?
Like, for you to be able to fact check every sentence in an article is just not realistic, as it is, you know, for most companies.
For you to be able to research.
Search, you know, a thousand different articles or a thousand different pages in order to come up with the best insights or the best information to use, that's also really time consuming, right?
And so what we want to do is make sure like these workflows are that they're that engine that takes great and figures out how to double down on what's great and how to scale that out, right?
And make it more reproducible, right?
And so that you can have more of a systematic execution.
It's not like you achieve great ones, good luck doing it again.
Right?
And then the last part is the human expertise.
And there's like different kind of pieces to the human expertise that I'll touch on in the kind of the three human roles, right?
And so think about it this way.
Service, workflow, expert, right?
And those are the three things we're building.
These are the three things we're good at.
These are the three things like that we sell, right?
Right?
And so then why the hell do we
We need us all here, right?
Like, why do we have all of us here, right?
And for the most part, this is, like, I'm focusing very much on the delivery team.
I'm not saying, like, every engineer needs to put into one of these three roles.
But when you think about it, every single person in the delivery team should fit into one of these three roles.
And it doesn't mean that's the only role you do, but it's very, very critical.
So the first one is process architects.

And so this is, these are the people that are going through and thinking through, like, what is the pattern, the thought process of getting the right inputs, processing those inputs, and how do you fill that out, right?
And so, for instance, like, if you think about how we're doing artifacts right now, right?
There is all these different ways we're doing research, and then we're processing those inputs, then we're taking, generating some artifacts.
Then we're going in, listen.
I to a call and then transcribing that call with the client, but then going and then taking the insights from that call and doing what we heard.
Then we take that and then reprocess the artifacts.
And, like, creating some of these loops is, like, helping us make sure that we're really knocking it out of the park for these artifacts, right?
So think of this as, like, who creates these blueprints, the place that we're going to run?
And there's not one single path to do these things.
There's multiple paths to do it, right?
And what we're doing is we're turning, like, best-in-the-world expert-level work into repeatable steps where it can be repeatable, right?
And then figuring out, like, how to write the documentation to scale it up, right?
So the success signal here is can you systematize messy, complex work into clear workflows and iterate over time?
Then this is...
Where I would say it's been one of the biggest gaps previously before the strategy sprints is the input calibrators.
And this is like, sometimes customers won't even tell you what you want to hear, right?
They'll tell you something else.
And it's like, how do we get the right inputs out of people?

And the more AI can do more things, the more this piece is going to be valuable of like asking really good questions, understanding what's behind the words that they're saying, understanding like what's important to them.
How do we prioritize that, right?
Like, how do we capture what they're trying to do?
How do we talk to different people within their organization to get that?
do we talk to their customers?
How do we ingest all those docs and things that they send our way?
And then like, how do we align expectations and make sure that we're, you know, all marching towards the same direction, right?
And do customers feel heard and understood?
What their outputs on brand and on strategy in over time delivering the right?

And so over and over again, like, especially in the listening tour, it's just so clear, you would just ask one of our customers something, and they would immediately, like, give you constructive feedback, and be, oh, and this, and this, and you're just like, whoa, like, I barely asked you a question, and you had, like, 50 things to share immediately.
It's like, well, why did it take, you know, like, six months for you to tell us this, right?
Like, so you just got to ask the right questions, get those inputs, that's our job, right?
And it's all about also, like, figuring out where the bar should be, so that the production refiners, right, are really doing this really critical job here, right?
And if you think about this third layer here, it's, like, taste is something that's really hard to teach, right?
But it's really critical, like, are we, every single output, when you share something with someone, right, and this happened with Surge, so we took our artifact process, and then we.

We had three different interviews with them, where we transferred out and took notes, and then we processed them, and then I did a one sentence prompt, where I just said, hey, write me a data quality manifesto, and I just spit out this thing, and then I shared with the CEO of Serge, and this is a guy that is literally the master sommelier of data, right?
Like, they're producing all, like, the best data possible, and he goes, whoa, this is so good.
I can't even tell if you guys use any LLMs for this.
This is so freaking good.
Wow, this is great.
Right?

And it wasn't because I had this crazy prompt, it was because, like, I asked right inputs, but then there's, like, this level of taste and insight, right, where it was like, hey, I know I have the right inputs, I know I have the right artifacts, now I understand their business really well, and it was like, like, I heard, what I heard was like, I didn't care about data, and I knew from my previous work that, like, when you use the word manifesto, you will write it in a certain way, and so just by doing that, all of a sudden, was like, this
Magical thing, right?
And then I wrote this other one, which is also a single-sentence prompt, which was, like, a love letter to human intelligence.
It was just, like, capturing the essence of what we heard from them, which was just, like, we're in love with human intelligence, and there's infinite depth to human expertise and intelligence.
It's not a finite thing that you can put into a bounding box.
And this whole idea of, like, bounding boxes kept coming up, and I was like, hey, all right, let's write a bounding box ballastise memo.
And then I wrote that, right?
And all of these was just, like, super, super brilliant and powerful.
But then there's, like, this, like, okay, like, you're applying all of these and having good taste and connecting the dots and sharing something that you're really proud of, right?
Like, and so it's still so critical, but also, like, this role has to raise the bar.
If you're in this role and you're reviewing content and the content is not getting substantially better, you're not doing, adding any value here, right?
And so for us, like...
It's our job to figure out, like, how do we evaluate that?
How do we understand and how do we help whoever is helping to refine this content and holding the bar?
You are the bar raiser of our outputs.
And it's so critical because you can either cause a customer to turn and be pissed or you can cause a customer to be over the moon, be like, this is amazing.
Holy smokes, like, this is amazing, right?
And so at the end of the day, like, it's all these three roles are really critical in very different ways.

But so, like, if we optimize for these three roles, we're going to continue to achieve what we want to achieve, which is, like, one, as long gets better, everything we do gets better, cheaper and faster.
And with every single output, it should make the next output cheaper, better, faster, right?
And if those two things are true as a company, we'll be in a really, really good place.
But it requires us to learn from everything.
And it requires us to have this mentality of building leverage in everything we do.
It completely defeats the purpose, by the way.
If all you're doing every day is copy and pasting  over to ChatGPT, and then pasting it over to a customer, it completely defeats the purpose of this company's existence.
We are not doing service because we just love working with really hard-to-please clients.
We're doing service because it's the fastest path to us learning and scaling this out with the highest amount of signals.

And so hopefully that helps, and I'll write more of these memos, but hopefully as we are shaping the roadmap of the Task Hub, and Atlas, and Studio, which we'll come up with a better name for all of them, but this kind of connects those dots.
So I know that was really long, but hopefully that's helpful, but please read the dots as well.
Bridget, back here.
All right, unless folks have questions.
Yeah, any questions?
We have a couple minutes.
No takers apparently have all my questions.
I think Tiandra asked a question earlier in the meeting.
If it's okay, I can read it.
She asked, as we scale up our clients, how will we approach managing the workload across pods?
Sorry, say that again.
So she asked, as we scale up our clients, how will we approach managing the workload across pods?
Yeah, so the, it's really about, like, understanding the inputs that drive the right outputs.
And with every rep, right, it's not about trying to distract the way of humans.
It's about, like, how do we, like, what, why did a client give this feedback?
Why was this on point?
What was this not on point?
What could we have done differently, right?
Like, and we're playing with that.
And it's really trying to figure out, like, how do we, you know, how does every week as things scale, it gets easier, not harder.
And if it's not getting easier, if you're working on a client output, and it's been weeks, and the amount of work to do the same output is the same or harder, like, we're doing something off.

And raise your hand, like, work with us, like, we'll figure it out, but the point is, it should be getting easier, it should be getting better, you know, and ideally it should be getting easier and better, you know, and so if it's getting worse and harder, which has happened quite a bit, then we're heading in the wrong direction, we need to think through, like, from our first principles, right?
And so, that's really what we've seen, is when you focus on the right first principles at the beginning, everything else later on gets better, right?

I'll give you an example, I talking to Mark from Rapid, and he's like, oh yeah, you know, there's just a lot of things that models are just not good at, there's all these gotchas, like, for instance, like, every piece of content you all send us mentions Stripe, and links to Stripe, which is, like, a competitor.
you very much.
And we just have to fix it every time, right?
And it's like, that comment, I was like, no, no, no, hold on.
Like, there's a workflow and a researcher where you can say not to focus on a domain, and we just need to do that.
It's like, oh, really?
Oh, there's all these, like, AI words I don't like that we use all the time.
It's like, yeah, like, we can actually put that into a rule of post-processing, right?
And he's super happy as a customer, so this is not, like, a thing going on, on, like, the team working on that account.

So then, like, if you're scrambling and you're like, man, I'm so underwater with the work, like, oftentimes there's, like, these things that we can just work a little bit smarter by using the systems we have and knowing how to use them, you know?
And so that's our goal is to make these systems more and more intuitive so that, you know, you don't need to be a PhD in order to know how to get out of them, you know?
Yeah, think, Mariana...

I think it's a great question, and maybe we can take it offline, too, and Panzer and others can kind of give you a little bit more guidance, too, on, like, where to put, like, competitors and things like that.

And I think we're probably also, like, overdue to just do a couple-hour session of just going through Atlas together, running some assignments together, you know, and Panzer and Tina spending a ton of time, especially with content managers, like, doing things live every day, and so we can do a lot more of those as well.
Cool.
Thank you, everyone.
Have a great week, and if you have any questions on any of this, just drop it in Slack somewhere.
All right.
Talk soon.
Thanks, everyone.
See next time.
Thanks.
Bye-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Bi-weekly all-hands meeting to review Q2 results, introduce new hires, showcase new logos, and discuss company strategy and frameworks.](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=60.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Q2 ended with $7.5M revenue run rate; strong growth but concerns about churn](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=397.0)
  - [New focus on deeper client understanding and high-quality outputs to build trust](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=447.0)
  - [Impressive new logos acquired (SentinelOne, Okta, Surge AI) with potential for co-marketing](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=591.0)
  - [New human-AI framework introduced to optimize delivery team roles and workflows](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1085.0)

## Topics

### Q2 Financial Results

  - [Revenue run rate: $7.5 million](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=397.0)
  - [Cash balance: Over $10 million](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=702.0)
  - Burn: Under $2 million (burn multiple \< 0.5)
  - [Presence in 22 countries](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=763.0)
  - [Concerns about churn and retention; addressing through improved client understanding and output quality](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=424.0)

### New Client Logos

  - [High-profile additions: SentinelOne, Okta, Surge AI](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=829.0)
  - [Surge AI deal: $60k/month + expenses, closed in 3 days](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=640.0)
  - [Other notable clients: Outreach (sales enablement platform), Monograph (tool for architects/engineers)](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=926.0)
  - [Pipeline includes Udbe, Ambien, Roar (AI/security focused companies)](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1000.0)

### Human-AI Framework

  - [Three layers: Service, AI Workflows, Human Expertise](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1132.0)
  - [Three key roles in delivery team:](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1231.0)
    1.  Process Architects: Create blueprints for workflows
    2.  Input Calibrators: Gather and process client inputs effectively
    3.  Production Refiners: Ensure high-quality outputs and raise the bar

### Strategy Sprints and Client Feedback

  - [Implementing deeper client understanding through extensive research and artifacts](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=447.0)
  - [Positive feedback on high-quality, insightful outputs (e.g., Surge AI data quality manifesto)](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1527.0)
  - [Focus on avoiding generic AI-generated content to maintain client trust](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=551.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Share detailed churn analysis document in the next two weeks](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=787.0)
  - [Conduct a session on effectively using Atlas for assignments](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1930.0)
  - [Continue to iterate on workflows to make each output cheaper, better, and faster](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1663.0)
  - [Ensure all team members understand and align with their roles in the human-AI framework](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1649.0)
  - [Maintain focus on learning from every output to continuously improve processes](https://fathom.video/share/F4jZnLyhbfWizxhx5sxFoAxvy-Sb2JFn?tab=summary&timestamp=1674.0)
